content: Devon flagged Voyage embedding API returning 429s during nightly compaction at ~2am UTC. Started around Mar 10, affecting memory persistence for all active agents. Hit rate tier was at 85K req/day; Voyage's burst limit ~500/min.
summary: Voyage 429s during nightly compaction—burst limit hit

content: Voyage pricing tiers: burst limit scales with plan. Standard tier caps at 500 req/min, Professional at 2K req/min. We're on Standard; compaction spike hits ~800/min. Cost difference $1200→$3800/mo. Devon recommended nightly reservation tier purchase instead.
summary: Voyage tier capacity and cost tradeoffs

content: When Voyage embedding API returns 429: implement exponential backoff starting at 2s, max jitter ±20%, cap at 5 retries. Devon added this to embedding_worker.py. Also stagger batch submissions across 3-min windows instead of dumping all at once.
summary: Implement exponential backoff for 429s; stagger batches

content: Anya asked if we should buy Professional tier or absorb backoff latency. Compaction normally takes 90min; with backoff, could stretch to 3.5hr, impacting agent memory freshness. Devon estimated implementation at 4 hours.
summary: Devon 4-hr backoff fix vs $2.6K/mo tier upgrade decision

content: Memory compaction runs nightly at 2am UTC, batching ~45K embeddings. Without throttling, hits burst limit consistently. Initiated by cron, processes via embedding_worker on three dedicated pods. Acme & Initech see stale memory if delayed >4hr.
summary: Nightly compaction: 45K embeddings, 2am UTC, burst sensitive

content: Approved Devon's approach: implement exponential backoff + purchase Voyage nightly reservation tier ($350/mo for guaranteed 3K req/min 2am-3am window). Backoff as fallback only. Took effect night of Mar 14.

Checked DataDog at 2:45am Mar 15. Voyage requests completing in 1.2s avg, no 429s. Devon's backoff + reservation tier working. Compaction finished 98min—normal. Flagged to Sebastien that incident resolved.
summary: Incident resolved: 429s gone, compaction back to baseline

content: Exponential backoff math: start 2s, multiply by 1.5 per retry, ±20% jitter, max 320s (5th retry). Theoretically handles transient 429s without DDoS-ing Voyage. Tested locally; Devon verified logic against Anthropic backoff spec.

Log all 429 incidents to Sentry with retry count, elapsed time, and batch ID. Monitor tail latency percentile on embedding_worker via DataDog (p99). If p99 >4min, escalate to Ari. Check weekly on Mondays.
summary: Monitor: Sentry for 429s, DataDog p99 latency, weekly review

content: I keep insisting on async RCA docs so I don't have to re-explain to Sebastien or Anya later. This time, Devon wrote two paragraphs in Linear ticket; saved 20min of repeat explanation. Wins like this compound.
summary: Async docs reduce context re-explaining—Devon did it right

content: Ran second nightly compaction successfully Mar 21. Voyage reservation tier auto-scaled to 3.1K req/min at 2am. No backoff triggered. Lina confirmed Initech & Wayne agents reporting fresh memory state. Cost impact: +$350/mo recurring.
summary: Reservation tier holding; Initech & Wayne memory freshness restored

content: Voyage incident reminded me: we're now at a scale where API burst limits matter. Compaction is synchronous to agent freshness. Margins are tighter than I assumed. Need to model capacity differently—not just per-agent, but per-batch. Ari should own this.
summary: At 60-person scale, synchronous batch limits matter; rethink capacity model
